coredata.dc:title: Quasi-homogenous dye-sensitized photocatalytic H2 evolution catalyzed by in-situ grown cobalt-promoted MoSx catalyst coupled with graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Yonggang
Xue, Yuan
Li, Yanan
Liu, Xiangyu
Deng, Wanan
Tian, Lei
Wang, Fang
Min, Shixiong
coredata.dc:description: The development of practical photocatalytic systems for large-scale and on-demand solar-to-hydrogen conversion requires that H2 evolution catalysts consist of earth-abundant materials and can be facilely obtained and modified. In this paper, we report a high-performance and quasi-homogenous dye-sensitized H2 evolution system by coupling graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with cobalt-promoted MoSx (Co-MoSx) catalyst. The Co-MoSx serving as H2 evolution catalyst can be facilely grown by a in-situ photochemical reduction method in the presence of an organic dye as the photosensitizer and simultaneously coupled with GQDs during photocatalytic reactions, forming GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst. The resulted GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst exhibits high activity for catalyzing H2 evolution under visible light due to the significant synergistic effects among GQDs, Co dopant, and MoSx. The doping of MoSx with Co ions results in an effective promotional effect on the H2 evolution activity of in-situ generated Co-MoSx catalyst, while the presence of GQDs can not only improve the dispersion of Co-MoSx catalyst by serving as a homogenous matrix but also lead to an efficient Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) to sensitizing dye by acting as a quasi-homogenous energy donor for enhancing light utilization efficiency. In addition, the GQDs can also act as an electron transfer relay to facilitate the electron transfer from excited dye to Co-MoSx catalyst, thus retarding the back electron transfer and reducing energy loss. This work provides a new route to construct an efficient and quasi-homogenous dye-sensitized H2 evolution system by integrating inexpensive MoSx-based catalyst with transition metal modification and highly photoluminescent and soluble GQDs with outstanding optical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quasi-homogenous
Graphene quantum dots
Cobalt-promoted MoSx
Dye-sensitization
H2 evolution

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In-situ synthesis of GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst and photocatalytic H2 evolution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Scientific Research Projects of the Higher Education Institutions of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region
Foundation of Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Conversion Technology
Foundation of State Key Laboratory of High-Efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21463001 and 21763001 ), the Key Scientific Research Projects of the Higher Education Institutions of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (Grant No. NCX2017143 ), the Foundation of State Key Laboratory of High-Efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering (Grant No. 2017-K26 ), the Key Scientific Research Projects in 2017 at North Minzu University (Grant No. 2017KJ20 ), and the Foundation of Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Conversion Technology and Application .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed mechanism of photocatalytic H2 evolution from ErB-GQDs/Co-MoSx-TEOA system under visible light irradiation.

TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of GQDs. Insets in panel (a) and (b) show the statistics of size distribution and the measured lattice distance of graphene (100) planes of a single GQD particle. (c) XRD pattern of GQDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of GQDs. (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs solutions at different concentrations. (f) Normalized PL emissions of GQDs excited at different wavelengths of light.

Average photocatalytic H2 evolution rate from ErB-TEOA system catalyzed by different catalysts. Reaction conditions: ErB, 1 mM; GQDs, 10 mg; MoSx, 8.98 × 10−5 M; Co, 5.98 × 10−5 M; 15 vol% TEOA, 80 mL, pH 7; light source: 300-W Xe lamp, ≥420 nm.

Average photocatalytic H2 evolution rate from ErB-TEOA system catalyzed by GQDs coupled Co, MoSx and Co-MoSx with different Co:Mo ratios. Reaction conditions: ErB, 1 mM; GQDs, 10 mg; 15 vol% TEOA, 80 mL, pH 7; light source: 300-W Xe lamp, ≥420 nm.

Average photocatalytic H2 evolution rate from ErB-GQDs/Co-MoSx-TEOA system as a function of ErB concentration. Reaction conditions: GQDs, 10 mg; MoSx, 8.98 × 10−5 M; Co, 5.98 × 10−5 M; 15 vol% TEOA, 80 mL, pH 7; light source: 300-W Xe lamp, ≥420 nm.

Average photocatalytic H2 evolution rate from dye-GQDs/Co-MoSx-TEOA system as a function of dye used. Reaction conditions: dye, 1 mM; GQDs, 10 mg; MoSx, 8.98 × 10−5 M; Co, 5.98 × 10−5 M; 15 vol% TEOA, 80 mL, pH 7; light source: 300-W Xe lamp, ≥420 nm.
UV–vis absorption spectra of TEOA solution of (a) GQDs/CoCl2/(NH4)2MoS4, (b) ErB/GQDs, (c) ErB/CoCl2/(NH4)2MoS4, and (d) ErB/GQDs/CoCl2/(NH4)2MoS4 under visible light irradiation (≥420 nm).

TEM (a, b) and HRTEM images (c, d) of GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst. Inset figure acrossing panels (a) and (b) shows the statistics of size distribution. (e) HAADF-STEM image of GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst and corresponding EDS mapping images. (f) XRD pattern of GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst. (g–i) High-resolution XPS spectra of (g) Mo 3d, (h) S 2p and (i) Co 2p core levels for GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst.

(a) GQDs absorption, ErB absorption, and GQDs emissions. (b) The quenching of PL emission of GQDs (40 μg mL−1) excited at 420 nm by adding ErB dye. (c) The quenching of PL emission of ErB (10 μM) solutions by GQDs. The excitation wavelength is 520 nm. (d) The quenching of PL emission of ErB (10 μM)/GQDs (40 μg mL−1) solutions by Co/Mo salt, where the Co:Mo ratio is 4:6. The excitation wavelength is 520 nm.

Wavelength-dependent AQY of H2 evolution fromErB-GQDs/Co-MoSx and ErB-Co-MoSx systems. Inset shows the enhanced factor for AQY by comparing both systems. Reaction conditions: ErB, 1 mM; GQDs, 10 mg; MoSx, 8.98 × 10−5 M; Co, 5.98 × 10−5 M; 15 vol% TEOA, 80 mL, pH 7; light source: 300-W Xe lamp equipped with different band-pass filters (420, 450, 475, 500, 520, 550, or 600 nm).

head.ce:title: Quasi-homogenous dye-sensitized photocatalytic H2 evolution catalyzed by in-situ grown cobalt-promoted MoSx catalyst coupled with graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Conversion Technology and Application, North Minzu University, Yinchuan, 750021, Ningxia Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Conversion Technology and Application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of practical photocatalytic systems for large-scale and on-demand solar-to-hydrogen conversion requires that H2 evolution catalysts consist of earth-abundant materials and can be facilely obtained and modified. In this paper, we report a high-performance and quasi-homogenous dye-sensitized H2 evolution system by coupling graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with cobalt-promoted MoSx (Co-MoSx) catalyst. The Co-MoSx serving as H2 evolution catalyst can be facilely grown by a in-situ photochemical reduction method in the presence of an organic dye as the photosensitizer and simultaneously coupled with GQDs during photocatalytic reactions, forming GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst. The resulted GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst exhibits high activity for catalyzing H2 evolution under visible light due to the significant synergistic effects among GQDs, Co dopant, and MoSx. The doping of MoSx with Co ions results in an effective promotional effect on the H2 evolution activity of in-situ generated Co-MoSx catalyst, while the presence of GQDs can not only improve the dispersion of Co-MoSx catalyst by serving as a homogenous matrix but also lead to an efficient Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) to sensitizing dye by acting as a quasi-homogenous energy donor for enhancing light utilization efficiency. In addition, the GQDs can also act as an electron transfer relay to facilitate the electron transfer from excited dye to Co-MoSx catalyst, thus retarding the back electron transfer and reducing energy loss. This work provides a new route to construct an efficient and quasi-homogenous dye-sensitized H2 evolution system by integrating inexpensive MoSx-based catalyst with transition metal modification and highly photoluminescent and soluble GQDs with outstanding optical properties.

ce:section.ce:para: H2 production via photocatalytic water splitting has been viewed as a promising route for converting massive solar energy to clean fuel and addressing increasing environmental problems [1]. Among various photochemical H2 production protocols, a dye-sensitized system composing of a photosensitizer (PS), a H2 evolution catalyst (HEC), and an electron transfer relay (ETR), has been established to be efficient strategy of carrying out water reduction to liberate H2 gas under visible light irradiation [2–11]. To achieve a high-performance and cost-effective H2 evolution from dye-sensitized systems, apart from using inexpensive organic dyes as photosensitizer instead of noble-metal containing complexes [12–20], the design and development of an advanced HEC that consists of earth-abundant elements and exhibits a low H2 evolution overpotential to replace scarce and high-cost Pt-group metals are great of importance [21,22]. In addition, developing an efficient ETR to facilitate the electron transfer from excited dyes to HEC is also crucial to achieve a high-performance dye-sensitized H2 evolution [2–13].

Up to now, a number of enzyme-like artificial HECs such as phosphides [23,24], nitrides [25,26], borides [27,28], carbides [29], selenides [30], and sulfides [31–41] of transition metals (Fe, Co, Ni, Mo, W, Cu, etc.) have been employed in photocatalytic H2 evolution and showed competed HER activity to Pt-group catalysts. Of note, the in-situ generated amorphous molybdenum sulfides (MoSx) in dye-sensitized systems or on the surface of semiconductors have been reported to show high H2 evolution activity [36–43]. Furthermore, the activity of amorphous MoSx for catalyzing H2 evolution can be further improved by in-situ modification with transition metal ions [37,41]. Although the aforementioned progresses have been achieved, most of the recent efforts have only focused on enhancing the H2 evolution activity of MoSx-based catalysts by optimizing metal ion doping or prolonging the stability of MoSx-catalyzed H2 evolution systems by combining with more stable semiconductors, and only a few of attempts have been devoted to improve the H2 evolution performances of dye-sensitized MoSx systems by modulating the electron transfer kinetics from excited dye to HEC via an efficient ETR. In the previous studies [5–20], several ETRs including large-surface-area semiconductors [5–12], metal-organic frameworks, and conductive carbon nanomaterials [13–15] have been incorporated into dye-sensitized systems to facilitate the electron transfer from excited dye to loaded HECs, and an obviously improved H2 evolution performance has been obtained. However, the heterogeneous feature of above-mentioned ETRs commonly leads to a nonuniformity of reaction system, leading to an energy loss of incident light due to the light scattering effect of the suspended ETR particles and reducing the utilization efficiency of active sites of loaded HECs on ETR support due to the blocking effects. Moreover, most of the previously studied ETRs usually acted as a dye loading matrix and an electron transfer relay, and the light-induced interplays between dyes and ETRs that will further enhance the H2 evolution performance have not been fully explored yet.

In this paper, we report a high-performance quasi-homogenous dye-sensitized photocatalytic H2 evolution system by coupling graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with in-situ generated amorphous Co doped MoSx (Co-MoSx) as an advanced H2 evolution catalyst. In this system, the water-soluble and highly photoluminescent GQDs could serve as a homogenous matrix to enhance the dispersion of Co-MoSx catalyst, act as an homogenous energy donor to transfer high-energy light photons to the sensitizing dye via an efficient Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process for maximizing light utilization efficiency of photocatalytic system, and act as an efficient ETR to accelerate electron transfer from excited dye to Co-MoSx catalyst for reducing energy loss, as a result, the thus-obtained in-situ coupled GQDs/Co-MoSx shows high activity towards H2 evolution under visible light irradiation. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first example of synergistically combining inexpensive MoSx-based catalyst with GQDs towards a highly active catalyst for catalyzing H2 evolution from a noble-metal-free dye-sensitized system. The essential roles of each component and the light-induced interplays between them for improving H2 evolution performance were investigated and a possible mechanism was also proposed.

All reagents including triethanolamine (TEOA), Erythrosin B (ErB), Eosin Y (EY), Rose Bengal (RB), Fluorescein Sodium (FS), Fluorescein Yellow (FY), (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, (NH4)2S solution (21%), and CoCl2·6H2O were of analytical grade and used without further purification. (NH4)2MoS4 was prepared from (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O and (NH4)2S solution according to a reported procedure [36]. Deionized (DI) water produced by an YL-100B-D water purification system was used throughout experiments.

The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized according to a reported procedure [44]. Briefly, 0.5 g of citric acid and 0.5 g of thiourea were dissolved into 20 mL of DI water under stirring. Then, the mixture solution was heated in a microwave oven (700 W) for 10 min under stirring at 450 rpm until the solution changed from colorless to dark brown, showing the carbonization of the reactants. After cooling to room temperature, 20 mL of DI water was added to dissolve the product. The as-obtained dark brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and then the large particles were successively filtered out through 0.22 μm and 25 nm filter membranes. Finally, the clear solution was concentrated with a rotary evaporator and then freeze-dried to get brown solids.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken with a Tecnai-G2-F30 field emission transmission electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Rigaku Smartlab diffractometer with a nickel filtrated Cu Kβ radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of the samples were performed on a K-Alpha surface analysis (Thermon Scientific) using X-ray monochromatization. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were performed on Thermo Nicolet Avatar 380 FT-IR spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a TU-1810 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Persee). Photoluminescence spectra were determined by a Horiba Scientific FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer. The fluorescence decay times were measured using the Horiba Jobin Yvon Data Station HUB operating in time-correlated single photon counting mode (TCSPC) with the time resolution of 200 ps. Nano LED diode emitting pulses at 370 and 454 nm with 1 MHz repetition rate was used as an excitation source. Light-scattering Ludox solution was used to obtain the instrument response function (prompt). The time ranges are 0.055 ns/channel in 4096 effective channels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ synthesis of GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst and photocatalytic H2 evolution
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst was synthesized in-situ during the photosensitized H2 evolution reaction, which was performed in a sealed quartz reaction cell with a top flat window for light irradiation and a silicone rubber septum was fixed on its side for sampling produced H2 in the headspace of reactor. In a typical procedure, the reaction mixture were prepared by sequentially dissolving calculated amount of GQDs, CoCl2·6H2O, (NH4)2MoS4, and ErB into 80 mL of 15 vol% TEOA aqueous solution (pH 7) in reaction cell under vigorous stirring. Before commencing light irradiation, the reaction system was thoroughly degassed by N2 bubbling to remove the oxygen, and then the system was irradiated by a 300-W Xe lamp (Beijing NBeT Co. Ltd., HXS-F300) equipped with an optical cut-off filter (λ ≥ 420 nm). During the light irradiation reaction, the reaction solution was continuously stirred and maintained at a constant temperature (∼20 °C) by a cold-water jacket. The amount of evolved H2 was determined using a gas chromatograph (GC-7900, Tech comp) with thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and N2 as carrying gas. At the end of H2 evolution reaction, the thus-obtained GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst was collected by dialysis and freezing-dried for further characterizations. Free MoSx or Co-MoSx catalyst was also obtained by the exact same procedures for GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst except the addition of GQDs and Co salt or GQDs.

The monochromatic quantum yields for the H2 evolution were measured under the condition similar to the above photocatalytic reaction except the light source was equipped with various band-pass filters of different wavelengths. The photon flux of incident light was determined using a Ray virtual radiation actinometer (Apogee MQ-500, silicon ray detector, light spectrum, 389–692 nm; measurement range, 0–4000 μmol m−2 s 1). The apparent quantum yields (AQY) was calculated from the ratio of the number of reacted electrons during H2 evolution to the number of incident photons according to the following Eq. (1):
(1)
A Q Y ( % ) =
2 ×
number of evolved H
2
molecules
number of incident photons
× 100

GQDs were synthesized in large-scale (∼0.7 g) by a simple and fast microwave-assisted thermal condensation method using citric acid and thiourea as precursors [44]. The as-prepared GQDs are water-soluble and highly photoluminescent under UV light irradiation (365 nm), as shown in Fig. S1. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 a) shows that the GQDs are spherical quantum dots with a size distribution in the range from ∼2.5 to 6.7 nm and an average size of 4.4 nm (see inset in Fig. 1a). The high-resolution TEM image of single GQD particle in Fig. 1b indicates that the GQD is highly crystalline graphene crystals with a clear lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to that of graphene (100) planes [45,46]. The XRD pattern of GQDs (Fig. 1c) shows a characteristic of graphite with a (002) layer spacing identical to that of bulk graphite (∼0.35 nm), consistent with the previous observations on well-crystallized GQDs [45]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results (reported elsewhere) reveal that the GQDs are mainly composed of C (80.49 at.%) and O (15.73 at.%) elements and doped with small amount of N (3.14 at.%) and S (0.63 at.%) elements, indicating that the GQDs are dual-doped quantum dot materials. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 1d) shows the existence of a variety of conjugated groups such as CO, CC, CN, and CS and hydrophilic COO− and −OH/NH groups, which make the GQDs having a good optical properties and water-soluble. Fig. 1e shows the electronic absorption spectra of GQDs aqueous solutions at different concentrations. There is an obvious absorption peak centered at 339 nm, indicating the existence of conjugated carbonyl or carbonyl moieties [44], consistent with the FTIR results. The absorption intensity at 339 nm increase linearly with GQDs concentration (see inset in Fig. 1e), confirming that the GQDs exist as colloids in solution. Moreover, there is an enhanced absorption background from 400 to 600 nm for GQDs solution at higher concentrations, which is favorable to absorb visible light. Fig. 1f shows that the GQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) feature (Fig. S2), revealing that GQDs have a wide size distribution and different surface states due to the N and S doping, consistent with the TEM and XPS results. The excellent water solubility and highly photoluminescence would make GQDs to be an ideal matrix to construct a quasi-homogenous dye-sensitized photocatalytic H2 evolution system and study the light-induced interplays involved in the H2 evolution processes.

The effect of the molar ratio of Co to Mo and photosensitizer concentration was then investigated to optimize the catalytic activityof resulted GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst for H2 evolution from ErB-TEOA system. A series of GQDs/Co-MoSx catalysts were obtained from a solution containing CoCl2 and (NH4)2MoS4 with varied concentration ratios from 1:9 to 7:3 while the total molar concentration of Co and Mo and dosage of GQDs were fixed. As shown in Fig. 3 , the ratio of Co to Mo has a significant influence on H2 evolution activity, consistent with previous reports [37,41]. Specifically, an obvious enhancement in H2 evolution activity can be observed even by adding small amount of Co (Co:Mo, 1:9–3:7). The Co-MoSx catalyst at a Co to Mo ratio of 4:6 exhibits the highest activity, which is 3.3 and 6.1 times higher than GQDs/MoSx and GQDs/Co catalysts, respectively, clearly indicating that Co dopant is effective to promote the catalytic activity of MoSx and there is a synergistic effect between Co dopant and MoSx in GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst for achieving higher H2 evolution activity. With the further increase in Co:Mo ratio, the activity of resulted GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst decreases. At fixed Co-MoSx catalyst concentration and Co:Mo ratio (4:6), the variation of H2 evolution activity with ErB concentration was studied and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The activity maximizes at a ErB concentration of 2 mM, and higher ErB concentration (>2 mM) will lead to a decrease in activity, probably due to the excessed ErB in solution could be a filter of input light and cause the self-quenching of excited dye molecules [13–15]. Besides ErB, other xanthene dyes including Eosin Y (EY), Rose Bengal (RB), Fluorescein Sodium (FS), and Fluorescein Yellow (FY) were also employed as the photosensitizer to carry out visible-light-driven H2 evolution reactions catalyzed by GQDs/Co-MoSx from TEOA solution. The results in Fig. 5 indicate that ErB demonstrates the highest activity among these xanthene dyes with different halogen substitutions to the xanthene ring. In general, heavy-atom-substituted dyes such as ErB and RB, except EY, have higher activity than that of light-atom-substituted counterparts (FY and FS), which can be attributable to the heavy-atom effect that can facilitate the efficient formation of long-lived triplet states from their singlet states [4,14].

The in-situ formation pathway of GQDs/Co-MoSx in the presence of ErB under visible light irradiation was studied using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. Fig. 6 a shows that the (NH4)2MoS4 is unstable in the presence of Co ions and GQDs under visible light irradiation, consistent with our previous observation [37]. Moreover, the GQDs/CoCl2/(NH4)2MoS4 system shows no H2 evolution activity, indicating that the (NH4)2MoS4 decomposed into certain species that are inactive. Irradiation of ErB in the presence of GQDs (ErB/GQDs) leads to a rapid bleaching of ErB due to the ErB degradation as indicated in Fig. 6b. Also, as mentioned previously, GQDs cannot catalyze the H2 evolution (Fig. 2). After CoCl2 and (NH4)2MoS4 as the precursors of Co-MoSx catalyst were mixed with ErB and irradiated (Fig. 6c), the H2 evolution could be observed along with the disappearance of characteristic absorption peaks of (NH4)2MoS4 and a decrease in absorption intensity of ErB, indicating that (NH4)2MoS4 can be efficiently reduced to MoSx with concurrent modification with Co, leading to the formation of Co-MoSx catalyst for efficiently catalyzing H2 evolution reaction. This result also suggests that the coexistence of dye and MoSx catalyst is indispensable for H2 evolution. As to the system of ErB/GQDs/CoCl2/(NH4)2MoS4 under light irradiation, its UV–vis absorption features are similar to the ErB/CoCl2/(NH4)2MoS4 system (Fig. 6d), indicating that the presence of GQDs has no significant influence on the formation of Co-MoSx catalyst and the coupling of Co-MoSx with GQDs could be accomplished in one-step, resulting in the formation of GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst, which shows high activity for catalyzing H2 evolution.

The in-situ formed GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst was characterized by TEM, XRD, and XPS, as shown in Fig. 7 . The low-magnification TEM images in Fig. 7a and b reveal that GQDs/Co-MoSx particles are highly dispersed with an morphology similar to the GQDs. The particle size of GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst is in the broad range of ∼3.6–13.5 nm (see inset in Fig. 7a and b), which is much larger than that of GQDs (Fig. 1a), indicating the success in-situ coupling of Co-MoSx with GQDs during photocatalytic H2 evolution. The HRTEM images of GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst in Fig. 7c and d show that either the crystalline GQDs core were covered by amorphous Co-MoSx shell (Fig. 7c) as the size of GQDs is relatively large or the crystalline GQDs and the amorphous Co-MoSx are intimately coupled with each other with formation of multijunction (Fig. 7d) when the size of GQDs is relatively small. The high angle annular dark field-scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image and corresponding element mapping images (Fig. 7e) also clearly exhibit that C, N, and S elements from GQDs as well as Mo, S, and Co elements from Co-MoSx distribute uniformly throughout the sample, further demonstrating the strong coupling of the GQDs and Co-MoSx. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1f) of GQDs/Co-MoSx shows that apart from the characteristic diffraction peak of GQDs, an broad shoulder peak can also be clearly observed, probably due to the presence of amorphous Co-MoSx, further confirming the effective coupling of GQDs with Co-MoSx to form GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst, consistent with the TEM results. The XPS survey analysis indicates the existence of C, N, Mo, S, and Co elements in the GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst, again confirming the in-situ coupling of GQDs with Co-MoSx (Fig. S4). Fig. 7g–i shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of Mo 3d, S 2p, and Co 2p core levels for GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst. The Mo 3d5/2 (Mo 3d3/2) peak can be fitted into three doublet peaks, which can be ascribed to the presence of MoIVSx, MoVOSx, and a moderate amount of MoVIO3, respectively, consistent with the observations for amorphous MoSx prepared by electrodeposition or photodeposition method [37,39,41]. Deconvolution of S 2p envelope gives two main doublets and one doublet at higher binding energies, indicating the existence of both monosulfide S2− and disulfide S2 2−ligands [37] as well as sulfate. The fitting of Co 2p3/2 peak reveals several Co species with different chemical environments. The Co 2p3/2 peaks at 780.8 and 782.6 eV are ascribed to CoOx/Co(OH)x species, while the peaks at 783.6–790.0 eV are assigned to satellite structures. Most importantly, a Co 2p3/2 peak at 779.2 eV is found, being very close to that reported for sulfided Co species in the form of “CoMoS” phase [41]. The existence of “CoMoS” phase would account for the obvious promotion effect for MoSx in catalyzing H2 evolution due to the activation of S-edges by modifying the hydrogen-bonding energy [37,41]. These results indicate that Co ions can be incorporated into the MoSx matrix in-situ in the presence of GQDs during the photocatalytic H2 evolution process, leading to the formation of strongly coupled GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst with intimate interface for fast electron transfer and abundant active sites for H+ adsorption and reduction, thus showing high-performance in catalyzing subsequent ErB-sensitized H2 evolution.

To better understand the important roles of GQDs for enhancing H2 evolution activity of GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst from homogenous ErB-TEOA system, the involved energy/electron transfer processes mediated by GQDs were investigated using PL spectroscopy. As aforementioned, the GQDs used in this study is water-soluble and shows outstanding light absorption and emission properties (Fig. 1), thus it could be expected that the GQDs could act as a quasi-homogenous energy donor that can effectively couple with sensitizing ErB dye to increase the light utilization efficiency of the H2 evolution system via a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism, like inorganic quantum dots and dyes did [48–53]. It has been recognized that efficient energy transfer from a donor to an acceptor based on FRET would occur if the emission spectrum of the donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor [48–54]. Fig. 8 a shows that the PL emissions of the GQDs excited upon visible light irradiation (420–500 nm) significantly overlaps with ErB absorption, thus the energy transfer from excited GQDs to ErB is thermodynamically feasible, where GQDs act as the energy donor and ErB as the acceptor, respectively. This efficient energy transfer process is further verified by the PL titration experiments as shown in Fig. 8b, where the PL emission of GQDs ranging from 440 to 540 nm (λ ex = 420 nm) could be gradually quenched with the increase in ErB concentration while a substantial increase in the ErB emission centered at ∼560 nm could also be observed. Since the ErB only exhibits weak emission in the absence of GQDs (Fig. S5), the enhancement of ErB emission in the presence of GQDs is indeed due to the energy transfer from GQDs to ErB [48,49]. In addition, the time-resolved PL (TRPL) spectroscopy was used to study the kinetics of this light-induced energy transfer process. As shown in Fig. S6, the emission lifetime of GQDs at 465 nm has no change in the presence of ErB, while the emission lifetime of ErB at 550 nm is increased in the presence of GQDs. The enhanced lifetime for singlet excited ErB (ErB1*) will significantly facilitate the intersystem crossing (ISC) to produce the low-lying triplet excited state (ErB3*). Since the ErB3* is the crucial species for H2 evolution reaction, it can be inferred that the longer lifetime the ErB1* is, the more the ErB3* produced, and the higher the H2 evolution activity obtained [13–15]. These results, therefore, clearly demonstrate that the GQDs can absorb high-energy photons to undergo a FRET to sensitizing ErB dye, in turn enhancing the overall light utilization efficiency to produce more electrons for carrying out efficient H2 evolution reaction over Co-MoSx catalyst [50–52]. Compared to organic dyes and heavy-metal containing quantum dots, the GQDs as a homogenous energy donor are promising because of the water-soluble feature, strong absorption towards short-wavelength photons, and high photostability [55,56].

In order to further verify the role of above proposed FRET-based mechanism in enhancing the H2 evolution performance, the AQYs of photocatalytic H2 evolution over ErB-GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst at different wavelengths of monochromatic incident light were measured, as shown in Fig. 9 . It is found that the AQYs of H2 evolution over ErB-GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst under the short-wavelength light irradiation, where the GQDs can be excited, are higher than that of under long-wavelength light irradiation, where only ErB can be excited, clearly demonstrating that the FRET between GQDs and ErB indeed occurs and is effective to enhance the H2 evolution activity. The highest AQY of 1.08% is obtained at 420 nm over ErB-GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst. In addition, as compared to GQDs-free catalyst, ErB-GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst shows a much higher AQY over the experimental wavelength range of 420–600 nm, and the enhanced factor of H2 evolution is more pronounced under short-wavelength light irradiation (see inset in Fig. 9). These results strongly suggest that the GQDs acting as energy donor can greatly contribute to the enhancement of H2 evolution efficiency of the ErB-GQDs/Co-MoSx via efficient excitation energy transfer from GQDs to ErB, where GQDs and ErB can synergistically absorb high-energy and low-energy photons, respectively, thus enhancing the light utilization efficiency and improving the activity [48–53].

Apart from as a homogenous energy donor, the GQDs could also be an electron transfer relay to facilitate electron transfer from excited ErB to Co-MoSx catalyst. Given that the reported reduction potential of ErB is −0.91 V vs. NHE [42], and the conduction band minimum (CBM) of GQDs is reported to be −0.65 V vs. NHE [57,58], thus the electron transfer from excite ErB (ErB*) to GQDs upon the light irradiation is thermodynamically feasible, which would render an efficient charge separation, and hence enhancing the H2 evolution activity of ErB-sensitized GQDs/Co-MoSx catalyst. In order to verify this hypothesis, the PL emission behaviors of ErB in the presence of different concentrations of GQDs were studied (Fig. 8c). It is clearly that the strong PL emission of ErB at ∼555 nm can be efficiently quenched by GQDs and the quenching efficiency of ErB increases with the increase in GQDs concentration, suggesting that the excited ErB can transfer electrons to GQDs effectively due to the superior electron accepting properties of GQDs [47]. Furthermore, the addition of Co/Mo salt could further enhance the quenching efficiency of ErB (Fig. 8d), indicating that the presence of GQDs would render the ErB-sensitized GQDs/Co-MoSx system with more favorable electron transfer kinetics for H2 evolution.

Based on above results and discussion, a plausible mechanism for enhanced H2 evolution activity of quasi-homogenous ErB-sensitized GQDs/Co-MoSx system is proposed, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Generally, there are two pathways involved in the H2 evolution processes. In the first pathway (pathway I), both GQDs and ErB could get excited upon visible light irradiation (≥420 nm) by absorbing high-and low-energy photons, respectively. Then, the excitation of GQDs can undergo an energy transfer to ErB via FRET, as a result, the overall utilization of incident light would be enhanced, thereby enhancing the H2 evolution performance of this system. Moreover, since the FRET requires neither physical contact nor charge exchange for the donor and acceptor [48], the efficient energy transfer between water-soluble GQDs and ErB would be expected to occur at a long-range distance because of the strong spectral overlap of both and high emission efficiency of GQDs. In the second pathway (pathway II), the GQDs, as a building block of graphene, could also serve as an efficient electron acceptor and transporter to trap excited electrons of ErB and then transfer to Co-MoSx catalyst, which will effectively retard the back electron transfer for reducing energy loss, as a result of greatly improving the H2 evolution activity of ErB-sensitized GQDs/Co-MoSx system. In addition, GQDs are highly soluble in the reaction solution, making the H2 evolution system quasi-homogenous, which is favorable to enhance the utilization efficiency of active sites of dispersed Co-MoSx catalyst and light absorption efficiency by reducing light scattering effect.

In summary, we have successfully demonstrated a concept of constructing quasi-homogenous dye-sensitized photocatalytic H2 evolution system by employing water-soluble and highly photoluminescent GQDs as the homogenous energy donor and electron transfer matrix, and inexpensive in-situ generated Co-MoSx as H2 evolution catalyst. The GQDs as the homogenous energy donor can absorb high-energy photons and undergo an efficient FRET to transfer the excitation to ErB acceptor for enhancing the light utilization efficiency. At the same time, GQDs can also act as an efficient electron transfer relay to facilitate the electron transfer from excited ErB to Co-MoSx catalyst, reducing the energy loss to enhance H2 evolution activity. In addition, the in-situ modification of MoSx with Co ions will greatly enhance the catalytic activity of resulting Co-MoSx catalyst. As a result, the ErB-sensitized GQDs/Co-MoSx system exhibited much higher activity for H2 evolution due to the strong synergistic effects of GQDs, Co dopant, and MoSx. This work provides a simple and effective strategy to develop a high-performance noble-metal-free dye-sensitized H2 evolution system by integrating inexpensive MoSx-based catalyst with multifunctional GQDs, which holds great potentials for applications in solar energy conversion systems and devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21463001 and 21763001), the Key Scientific Research Projects of the Higher Education Institutions of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (Grant No. NCX2017143), the Foundation of State Key Laboratory of High-Efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering (Grant No. 2017-K26), the Key Scientific Research Projects in 2017 at North Minzu University (Grant No. 2017KJ20), and the Foundation of Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Conversion Technology and Application.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.08.025.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article.
